case.title: MSP Midnight Breach Compare | MiniMax Provider-Owned
case.objective: You are the only MSP security engineer awake. Twelve SMB clients report PowerShell spawning from the RMM agent minutes after a new automation package was pushed. Give the first-hour response plan.
constraints: Answer in a single assistant voice.
Keep the plan factual, methodical, and sequenced.
Use decision gates before destructive action.

answers.label: ParaLLM MiniMax fast workers
answers.text:
**First-Hour Incident Response: RMM-Agent PowerShell Spawning (12 Clients)**

**T-0 to T-10: Immediate Triage and Evidence Preservation**

Do not mass power off any systems—process termination alone does not revoke RMM agent access and will destroy any volatile evidence you need to determine scope. Begin by isolating the problem: pull the RMM platform console and confirm which clients are actively reporting the behavior. Log into the automation package deployment history and flag the specific package deployed in that window. Snapshot current PowerShell process trees via RMM on the affected endpoints before taking any remediation action. Preserve those logs first—they answer whether this is a one-shot script or a persistent implant.

**T-10 to T-25: Containment Without Blind Isolation**

For non-24/7 clients, place affected endpoints in network quarantine through the RMM platform if the platform supports per-endpoint network isolation. For 24/7 operations, escalate the RMM agent's execution policy to block rather than terminate the PowerShell process, preserving the process for live forensics while preventing re-execution. Do not push a credential revocation across all tenants yet— blanket revocation may disrupt 24/7 services without evidence that credential compromise is the actual vector. Instead, identify whether the RMM backend is a shared multi-tenant system or tenant-segmented, because that single fact determines whether you are managing a targeted package compromise or an MSP-wide breach.

**T-25 to T-45: Forensic Evidence Collection**

Target three pieces of evidence urgently: (1) the full command line and parent process ID of the spawned PowerShell instances to confirm they launched from the RMM agent rather than a user-triggered schedule, (2) any new scheduled tasks, services, or registry Run keys created post-deployment that would indicate persistence, and (3) the contents of the automation package script itself against a known-good version in your version control. If the package was externally sourced, treat it as untrusted until hash verification completes.

**T-45 to T-60: Decision Gate—Wake Senior Lead?**

Wake the senior lead now if any of the following are true: the RMM backend is shared across all 12 reporters (indicating MSP-wide exposure), the PowerShell activity shows outbound network connections to external IPs, or the automation package originated from a third-party vendor with MSP-level access. If none of these conditions are met, continue investigation with a status update to the senior lead, but do not wait beyond the next 30 minutes to escalate if new indicators appear.

**Customer Communication**

Notify affected clients now with a brief factual statement: a software update triggered unexpected activity; you are investigating; no action is required from their end unless they observe further anomalies. Preserve trust by not speculating about breach scope before you have log confirmation. Reassure 24/7 clients that you are maintaining their operations continuity while containing the risk.

answers.label: ParaLLM MiniMax full triple
answers.text:
You are the only MSP security engineer awake. Twelve SMB clients report PowerShell spawning from the RMM agent minutes after a new automation package was pushed. Give the first-hour response plan.

This is the commander's first-pass answer direction before adversarial pressure.

answers.label: Direct MiniMax flagship
answers.text:
Suspicious PowerShell Execution from RMM Agent Post-Automation Deployment

HIGH - Active Post-Compromise Behavior

12

Minutes after new automation package deployment

Behavior is anomalous but root cause (compromised package vs. RMM agent vs. credential exposure) is unconfirmed. Assume credential compromise-level severity until disproven.

Halt propagation without blind mass shutdown

1

Immediately disable/pause the offending automation package in RMM console

If the package is the vector, this stops propagation to remaining clients.

Confirm no new executions in monitoring dashboard before proceeding

2

Enable RMM agent process monitoring/alerting if not already active

Must detect any resumed or secondary execution attempts

3

Isolate affected endpoints in RMM using network isolation policies (quarantine VLAN or client-side network cut) on a per-client basis

Avoids blind mass power-off; preserves evidence on disk; surgical approach respects 24/7 ops

Notify on-call client contacts before isolation for 24/7 operations unless delay risks lateral movement

Capture volatile and non-volatile evidence before remediation

Forensically capture PowerShell transcription logs, script block logs, and Windows Event Logs (4688, 4103, 4104) via RMM for all affected endpoints

Secure, immutable evidence store isolated from customer networks

Screenshot and hash the deployed automation package content from RMM console

Establishes baseline for malicious code comparison; proves chain of custody

Collect RMM agent logs and any executed script payloads from affected endpoints

Use read-only collection methods; do not terminate running processes until evidence captured unless process is actively malicious

Determine root cause vector before expanding containment scope

Compare hash and content of automation package across affected vs. unaffected clients (if any remain)

Identical malicious package = package is vector. Varied execution = RMM agent or credential compromise

Review RMM console for unauthorized package creation/modification events around deployment window

New package creation by non-standard account, API key usage anomalies

Query EDR/antivirus telemetry on affected endpoints for parent-child process relationships of suspicious PowerShell invocations

If parent process is consistently RMM agent across all 12 clients, this shifts hypothesis toward agent compromise vs. package compromise

WAKE SENIOR LEAD IMMEDIATELY if: (a) RMM agent is confirmed as consistently malicious parent, OR (b) PowerShell executions involve credential theft/injection behavior, OR (c) any affected client shows evidence of lateral movement to other systems

Preserve trust through transparent, factual notification

Send initial status notification to all 12 affected client contacts within 30 minutes

Acknowledged active investigation

Affected systems being monitored/isolated

Next update ETA (30-minute window)

Offer for direct call to 24/7 operations clients

Document all client notifications with timestamp and recipient

Required for liability documentation and SLA compliance

Decide whether to broaden scope before Hour 1 ends

If analysis confirms package as sole vector and affected clients are isolated, scope remains narrow. If RMM agent or credential compromise is confirmed, escalate to full RMM platform lockdown and credential rotation immediately.

Do not mass power-off systems. Do not rotate credentials until evidence captured from RMM console (rotation destroys authentication evidence).

Mass shutdown destroys evidence, disrupts 24/7 client operations, and may violate SLA/customer trust without confirmed justification. Surgical isolation via RMM network policies achieves containment with evidence preservation.

False

Pending until Phase 3 analysis complete. Wake if any Phase 3 trigger conditions met.

Automation package disabled in RMM

All 12 endpoints isolated or under active monitoring

Evidence captured from at least 3 representative endpoints

Customer notifications sent to all 12 clients

Initial root cause hypothesis documented

Confirm root cause and execute either targeted cleanup (package vector) or platform-wide remediation (RMM/credential compromise)
